paragraphs.paragraph_text: Annals of Oncology 17 (Supplement 2): ii5–ii10, 2006 doi:10.1093/annonc/mdj910 symposium article Small cell lung cancer G. Rosti1*, G. Bevilacqua2, P. Bidoli3, L. Portalone4, A. Santo5 & G. Genestreti5 1Medical Oncology, Ravenna; 2Surgical, Molecular and Ultrastructural Pathology, Pisa; 3National Cancer Institute, Milan; 4Sixth Pneumology Unit Forlanini Hospital, Rome; 5Medical Oncology Verona, Italy Small cell lung cancer accounts for 13–15% of all lung cancer worldwide. There has been a decrease in the number of cases, with no clear explanation, except probably to changing in smoking habits in the last two decades. In the early eighties, it became clear that SCLC was an extremely sensitive tumor as to radiation as to chemotheraputic agents. With cisplatinum etoposide combinations or cyclophosphamide, anthracycline and vincristine/etyoposide regimens responses were observed in 50–70%, with 20–30% complete remissions in extensive disease. For limited stage patients chemotherapy associated with thoracic radiation was able to produce a cure rate of 10–20%.The addition of prophylactic brain irradiation to limited stage cases has reduced mortality by a factor of nearly 5%. But despite these early good results no breakthrough came later on, and in the last decade or so, we are still facing this plateau. New agents have recently been included in the therapeutic armamentarium, such as gemcitabine, irinotecan, paclitaxel. This fact has allowed many patients to receive a relatively active second line therapy, but the overall survival remains unchanged. Targeted therapies are undergoing some evaluations, but the data are too premature and so far quite discouraging. At the present time there is a urgent need to improve clinical research in this somehow forgotten disease. Key words: small cell lung cancer, chemotherapy, radiotherapy biopathology Differently from the squamous histotype, for SCLC a clear pathogenesis is not recognized; pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia represents the only hypothetical precursor. This explains the absence of knowledge about the transforming events and the limited information existing about the genetics of SCLC progression. However, an origin of SCLC from the pulmonary neuroendocrine cells, which function as oxygen sensors, is hypothesized. ASH1 transcription factor, critically required for the formation of neuroendocrine cells, is expressed by SCLC cells and its expression level correlates with neuroendocrine markers as gastrin•releasing peptide, L•dopa•decarboxylase activity, calcitonin. Similarly, GFI1, required for differentiation and maturation of pulmonary neuroendocrine cells, is expressed in SCLC and its expression strongly correlates with the expression of ASH1, synaptophysin and chromogranin A. 85% of SCLC produces at least one of these three proteins: gastrin•releasing peptide (human homologue of amphibian bombesin), neuromedin B and their receptors, as bombesin receptor subtype 3. The secretion of the peptides and subsequent binding to their receptors leads to receptor activation and an autocrine•stimulated growth loop. Similarly, c•kit receptor is found in up to 70% of SCLC and is overexpressed and phosphorilated in presence of its ligand stem cell factor. The ligand is produced by tumor cells, binds to the receptor and causes its activation. Moreover, c•kit mutations have been identiﬁed in about 10% of SCLC. Both these situations are example of the existence of autocrine growth loops in the pathogenesis of SCLC, which represents important target for speciﬁc therapies based on antibodies or molecular inhibitors. The myc oncogenes (cmyc, nmyc, lmyc) encode nuclear phosphoproteins able to increase cell growth and cause cell• cycle progression. Myc proteins are overexpressed in 15–30% of SCLC as consequence of gene ampliﬁcation. A combination treatment of c•myc antisense DNA and all•transretinoic•acid is able to inhibit cell proliferation by downregulating myc expression. Chromosomal deletions affect 3p, 4p, 5q, 16q, 13q, 17p. 3p deletion is found in more than 90% of SCLC. Relevant genes are located on 3p: FHIT (fragile histidine triad), RASSF1 (the ras effector homologue), RARB (retinoic acid receptor beta), FUS1. p53 and Rb1 mutations are very frequent in SCLC: 75% and more than 90% respectively. PTEN LOH is also quite frequent, but its homozygous deletion is rare. The receptor tyrosine kinase c/met is implicated in SCLC, mainly through gain•of•function mutations in the juxtamembrane (JM) domain. EGF receptors are rarely expressed in SCLC, whereas Bcl2 gene is expressed in 75% of cases. Angiogenesis mediated by VEGF plays a role in SCLC progression, with a higher vascularization than NSCLC. For limited stage patients chemotherapy associated with thoracic radiation was able to produce a cure rate of 10–20%.The addition of prophylactic brain irradiation to limited stage cases has reduced mortality by a factor of nearly 5%.
But despite these early good results no breakthrough came later on, and in the last decade or so, we are still facing this plateau.
New agents have recently been included in the therapeutic armamentarium, such as gemcitabine, irinotecan, paclitaxel.
This fact has allowed many patients to receive a relatively active second line therapy, but the overall survival remains unchanged.
Targeted therapies are undergoing some evaluations, but the data are too premature and so far quite discouraging.

This explains the absence of knowledge about the transforming events and the limited information existing about the genetics of SCLC progression.
However, an origin of SCLC from the pulmonary neuroendocrine cells, which function as oxygen sensors, is hypothesized.

ASH1 transcription factor, critically required for the formation of neuroendocrine cells, is expressed by SCLC cells and its expression level correlates with neuroendocrine markers as gastrin•releasing peptide, L•dopa•decarboxylase activity, calcitonin.

Similarly, GFI1, required for differentiation and maturation of pulmonary neuroendocrine cells, is expressed in SCLC and its expression strongly correlates with the expression of ASH1, synaptophysin and chromogranin A.
85% of SCLC produces at least one of these three proteins: gastrin•releasing peptide (human homologue of amphibian bombesin), neuromedin B and their receptors, as bombesin receptor subtype 3.
The secretion of the peptides and subsequent binding to their receptors leads to receptor activation and an autocrine•stimulated growth loop.
Similarly, c•kit receptor is found in up to 70% of SCLC and is overexpressed and phosphorilated in presence of its ligand stem cell factor.
The ligand is produced by tumor cells, binds to the receptor and causes its activation.
Moreover, c•kit mutations have been identiﬁed in about 10% of SCLC.

Both these situations are example of the existence of autocrine growth loops in the pathogenesis of SCLC, which represents important target for speciﬁc therapies based on antibodies or molecular inhibitors.

3p deletion is found in more than 90% of SCLC.
Relevant genes are located on 3p: FHIT (fragile histidine triad), RASSF1 (the ras effector homologue), RARB (retinoic acid receptor beta), FUS1.
p53 and Rb1 mutations are very frequent in SCLC: 75% and more than 90% respectively.
PTEN LOH is also quite frequent, but its homozygous deletion is rare.

paragraphs.paragraph_text: in terms also of overall survival. Angiogenesis is also induced by MMP (matrix metalloproteinases), produced and released by SCLC cells. VEGF haplotypes of three polymorphisms (460T>C, +405C>G, 936C>T) are associated with the risk of SCLC, suggesting that VEGF gene may contribute to an inherited predisposition to lung cancer. YKL•40, a protein with a possible role in tissue remodelling, angiogenesis and cell proliferation, has elevated serum levels in advanced SCLC. This protein is produced by peritumoral macrophages. The increase of knowledge about the molecular mechanisms of SCLC initiation and progression is the only way for new therapeutic approaches, to improve the results of chemotherapy or to design ‘drugs’ for speciﬁc molecular targets, and for new tools for an early diagnosis. treament of limited disease (LD) Limited disease (LD) in SCLC is disease conﬁned to one hemi• thorax (but may include contralateral mediastinal lymph node involvement), in the absence of malignant effusion, and which can be encompassed in one radiation port. Disease not meeting these criteria is extended disease. About 30% of SCLC patients present with LD and 80–90% of these respond to combination chemotherapy, with or without thoracic radiation. In turn 40%–70% achieve complete remission, with median survival of 12–20 months, and 2• and 5•year survival of 20–40% and 5–10%, respectively [1, 2]. Although chemotherapy achieves high response rates, its use alone is associated with fairly high intrathoracic recurrence rates. Thoracic irradiation at doses not inferior to 40 Gy can induce local response, but by itself is unable to achieve good disease control. Combination therapy, consisting of thoracic irradiation and chemotherapy, produced better survival than chemotherapy alone in some trials [3, 4], although other trials using cyclophosphamide•based therapy failed to show a survival beneﬁt when irradiation was added [5]. Two meta•analyses carried out to resolve these conﬂicting results found a survival beneﬁt when conventionally fractionated thoracic radiation was added. One of these, which analysed 11 randomized trials, found that two•year survival improved by 5.4% and that intra•thoracic disease control improved by 25.3% in patients receiving chemotherapy and thoracic irradiation [6]. The other meta• analysis of 13 randomized trials found a 14% reduction in mortality, with 3•year overall survival improving by 5.4% in combined modality patients of age <55 yars [7]. These small survival advantages were found with old regimens containing cyclophosphamide plus adriamycin; however more recent studies indicate that etoposide plus platinum produce better survival [8] and less oesophageal toxicity than old regimens [9]. The addition of a third drug (e.g. taxol) to the etoposide•platinum plus irradiation combination was not able to improve survival [10]. As a result of these studies, combined modality therapy emerged as the accepted standard for patients with LD SCLC. However neither optimal radiotherapy dose nor optimal sequencing schedule has been established [11]. With regard to the relative timing of irradiation and chemotherapy (Table 1), early irradiation, (given concomitantly with the ﬁrst or second chemotherapy cycle) was thought better than later irradiation, principally based on signiﬁcant superiority reported in one Canadian trial [12]. However the preliminary data of a more recent British trial of identical design found no survival difference between early (course two) and late (course six) concurrent irradiation [13]. The issue of the superiority of concurrent versus sequential treatments is complicated by the heterogeneity of the chemotherapy regimens and irradiation schedules used, and lack of adequate power of most studies. Since SCLC is highly sensitive to low dose irradiation, hyperfractionation has seemed an attractive option. A Japanese study investigated early versus late twice•daily irradiation, ﬁnding a trend towards greater beneﬁt with earlier concurrent treatment [14]. Two other studies compared twice• daily with once•daily irradiation, ﬁnding increased survival with hyperfractionation in one case [9] and no difference in the other [15]. In both these studies, however, the hyperfractionation was associated with greater toxicity than standard once•daily irradiation. A recent systematic review of seven randomized trials with study populations ranging from 81 to 335 patients (total 1524), evaluated the timing of irradiation in SCLC. A signiﬁcant (P = 0.03) survival beneﬁt of 5% at two years was found for early (<9 weeks from start of chemotherapy) compared to late (>9 weeks) irradiation. The survival difference was similar at 3 years but no longer signiﬁcant, probably because of insufﬁcient power. Subset analysis found greater survival beneﬁt for twice•daily irradiation associated with platinum•based chemotherapy [16]. Note that the recent large British trial, which found no survival beneﬁt for early irradiation, [13] was not included in this meta•analysis as it has not been published in a peer• reviewed journal. Not unexpectedly, when the results of this trial were considered, the survival improvement at 3 years was much less evident. From the available data we can conclude that radiotherapy given concurrently with platinum and etoposide might be the best treatment currently available for LD SCLC. Early thoracic radiotherapy seems, on balance, better than late radiotherapy, however not all studies support this. For the present hyperfractionation should be conﬁned to the trial setting. Treatment•related toxicity such as esophagitis is worse with combination treatments but is acceptable in patients with adequate performance status. With regard to irradiation dose, the available data indicate that not less than 40 Gy in 15 daily fractions administered over 3 weeks (or biologically equivalent dose) may provide the best chance of local control; although the optimal dose has not been ascertained. When the disease in conﬁned to one hemithorax without mediastinal lymph node involvement (T1•2 N0) surgery appears better than radiotherapy [2] but must always be combined with adjuvant chemotherapy. Phase II trials have shown that both pre and post•chemotherapy surgery are feasible, with ﬁve•year survival in the range 10% to 50% depending on the extent of the primary [17]. paragraphs.paragraph_text: prophylactic cranial irradiation, a meta•analysis of seven trials (987 complete remission patients) found a 5.4% survival increase at 3 years (20.7 % versus 15.3%, P = 0.01) together with a reduction in incidence of brain metastases from 59% to 33% [18]. This study also found trends towards decreased brain relapse with larger radiation dose (30–36 Gy), and earlier administration (within 6 weeks of completion of primary chemotherapy). This analysis established prophylactic cranial irradiation as a reasonable option for LD SCLC, but indicated that it should only be given to patients in complete remission and should not be given concurrently with chemotherapy. No effect on cognitive function was observed in large trials [19]. In conclusion radiotherapy concurrent with platinum and etoposide, followed by prophylactic cranial irradiation is the current standard of care for LD•SCLC, and can provide a cure in 15%–25% of patients. Early concurrent radiotherapy gives a small (5%) long•term survival advantage compared to sequential radiotherapy but is associated with increased toxicity that may be acceptable in patients with adequate performance status. Hyperfractionated radiotherapy may provide a small additional beneﬁt, but should be conﬁned to the trial setting until this beneﬁt is conﬁrmed. treatment of extended disease (ED) Despite the high sensitivity to chemotherapy, nearly all patients will suffer from the development of chemotherapy resistance and will ultimately die from their disease: indeed long survivors are not more than about 15–20%. SCLC in limited stage disease. Disease beyond the thorax is present at diagnosis in more than 60%–70% of cases, and median survival for such patients is about 9.3 months, while 5•yr survival is 2% and 1% at 10 years. Chemotherapy is the therapy of choice for ED SCLC. From the 70’s multi agent chemotherapy approach has been the standard: early trials showed high chemosensitivity and, in the same time, more efﬁcacy for multi agent regimens than single agent ones, in term of response rate and overall survival. Platinum based chemotherapy is the mainstay of extensive SCLC treatment for the American Thoracic Society 2003 guide lines. This choice is based on two large meta•analyses which showing that cisplatinum based therapy (preferably with etoposide) is the cornerstone of the ED SCLC therapy. But we must note that three different studies performed by Roth, Fukuoka and Sundstro¨m, comparing the activity of cisplatinum and etoposide with the cyclophosphamide, adryamicin or epirubicin and vincristine regimen found a better activity in term of RR and OS for the cisplatinum regimen in Limited Disease, but the same activity in Extended Disease. The use of carboplatin in substitution of cisplatinum does not lead to a lack of activity of the regimen. In some trials, higher dosage vs a standard regimen has been investigated. Three studies have been performed using standard vs high dosage CAV therapy. In all the trials the investigators did not reach different results between two arms, with a substantial equivalence of the regimens. Toxicity was different, and it was more severe in higher dosage arms. No advantage was found in comparing higher versus standard doses of cisplatin etoposide combination. In higher dose arm toxicity was more pronounced. A recent review performed by Pasini et al., took into consideration 18 trials with a late dose intensiﬁcation and 8 with early intensiﬁcation, with the support of hematopoietic progenitors [20]. A ﬁrm conclusion has not been reached and high•dose approach has to be considered as an experimental approach. An important question regards the reduction of treatment intervals in order to intensify drug doses. After a positive ﬁnding [21], other trials were not able to demonstrate that shorter intervals improve OS. The same indifferent results were obtained adding third or forth drug to standard therapy. In many trials they obtained a longer time to progression and, some time, an improvement of OS. But the cost was higher in term of toxicity, so ATS guideline does not recommend an extended use of three or four drugs. Another important question is about the duration of therapy and if could be important a maintenance therapy. Sculier [22] performed a review in 1998 showing that 4 or 6 cycles are sufﬁcient to obtain longest survival, but a longer treatment could improve progression free survival. Near 50% of SCLC occurs in elderly people, and age related co morbidities make more difﬁcult treatment of disease. When we are treating a ﬁt people the choice is the same treatment we use in young people, but in other situation the choice of reducing drugs dosage to permit a better tolerance is not useful. In conclusion at this point we have not a regimen of choice in elderly and we are waiting for ﬁndings of G•STEP and other trials with biological agents who can permit a better tolerance with an improvement of survival. second•line treatment in Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) Treatment of relapse or refractory small cell lung cancer (SCLC) is a very difﬁcult problem for medical oncologists. After conclusion of a ﬁrst•line therapy, about 80% of SCLC patients with limited disease (LD) and about the totally of patients with extended disease (ED) will develop a relapse or progression disease [23]. In these patients the prognosis is very poor, in particular in elderly patients or patients with severe comorbidities where it is difﬁcult to propose an aggressive approach. Thus, it is important to respond to two questions: when and which regimen to use in patients with relapse or refractory SCLC. Predictive factors of response and prognostic factors to second•line chemotherapy are respectively: the response (complete or partial response) and the duration of response after ﬁrst•line chemotherapy (>9 months), the performance status (0–2) and the time off chemotherapy (>3 months). SCLC patients relapsing after at least 3 months by the conclusion of ﬁrst•line therapy (chemosensitive relapse) have a good chance to respond to the same induction regimen; therefore in patients with progression disease during the ﬁrst• line therapy or with relapse within three months by the conclusion of induction treatment (primarily refractory), it may be useful to use not cross•resistance drugs or new drugs combinations [24]. Cisplatin associated to etoposide (PE) used as second•line chemotherapy after failure of Cyclophosphamide/Adriamicyn/ Vincristine (CAV) produces response rate of 25–30%; but a second•line chemotherapy with CAV after PE produces a response•rate of 8–19%; both second•line chemotherapies produce a time to progression and a median survival (10–20 weeks) disappointed with an high rate of adverse effects [25]. In clinical practice, last generation drugs including topoisomerase I inhibitors (Topotecan, Irinotecan), semisynthetic vinca alkaloids (Vinorelbine), novel antimetabolites (Gemcitabine) and members of taxane family (Docetaxel, Paclitaxel) have stimulated the ﬁndings of new drugs regimen with a better therapeutic index. In particular Topotecan in monochemotherapy or in association with Cisplatin, Etoposide or Paclitaxel showed a good toxicity proﬁle and an antitumor activity in both chemosensitive and refractory disease (response rate 7–27%; median survival 16.3–30.1 weeks). Additionally, Topotecan demonstrated signiﬁcant symptom palliation in this patient population [26]. Based on these results, a phase III randomized study was planned where Topotecan was compared to CAV in 211 SCLC patients relapsing within 2 months from the conclusion of ﬁrst•line chemotherapy. About hematological toxicity, neutropenia was more frequent in the CAV arm while asymptomatic thrombocytopenia was reported in the Topotecan arm. Response rate, time to progression and overall survival were similar between the two treatments (24%, 3.3 and 5.8 months respectively for Topotecan; 18%, 3 and 5.5 months respectively for CAV). The authors concluded observing that both treatments were well tolerated, but a better clinical beneﬁt was registered in patients treated with Topotecan [27]. Based on these results, Topotecan in monochemotherapy is the standard treatment in second•line setting of SCLC patients in several nations, in particular USA. Oral Topotecan seems very interesting in patients with relapsed SCLC, poor prognosis and not eligible for intravenous chemotherapy. O’Brien et al. at the 2005 World Lung Cancer meeting presented the results of a phase III trial where 141 patients with relapse SCLC were randomized between best supportive care alone (70 patients) or in association to Topotecan 2.3 mg/m2/ day, d1•5 every 3 weeks (71 patients): the authors reported a signiﬁcant statistical beneﬁt in terms of overall survival and quality of life in favour to the arm with Topotecan [28]. Also Gemcitabine, a pyrimidine antimetabolite, has showed an antitumor activity both in vivo and in vitro against SCLC too: for its good toxicity proﬁle it may be associated to other chemotherapeutic agents active in the treatment of SCLC. In 2001 Domine reported an experience where Gemcitabine associated to Paclitaxel used in 31 pretreated SCLC patients produced 50% of response rate [29]. In 2003 Castellano et al. tested Gemcitabine associated to Irinotecan in 28 patients with relapsed or refractory SCLC with 50% of response rate [30]; disappointed results are reported with the combination of Gemcitabine with Docetaxel or Vinorelbine (response rate 0–6%) [31, 32]. Based on these results, treatment for subgroup patients with relapsed or refractory SCLC can extend survival, provide symptom palliation and produce quality•of•life beneﬁts. In particular for ‘chemosensitive’ patients with good performance status, it may be propose the same induction chemotherapy. For ‘refractory’ patients with performance status 0–2 it should considered to enroll the patients in clinical trials with non•cross resistance drugs. Finally for patients with performance status > 2 if ‘chemosensitive’ oral Topotecan may be a therapeutic chance, if the disease is chemoresistant the best supportive care should be considered as ﬁrst option. future directions Novel approaches employing target therapies have been extensively tested in non•small cell lung cancer in the last few years, while very few information have been produced in the ﬁeld of SCLC. The majority of SCLC expresses the c•KIT oncoprotein which is a member of the type III receptor tyrosine kinase family [33]. Anyway a careful study perfomed in the Netherlands has shown [34] that there is a lack of c•kit exon 11 activating mutations in c•kit/Cd117 positive sample. paragraphs.paragraph_text: imatinib is ineffective in this disease. A study performed on 26 patients (untreated or sensitive to prior chemotherapy) has shown no activity, with only one patient achieving stabilization [35]. This study anyway was conducted mainly in patients not showing expression of the imatinib target. In a recent study performed on twelve patients [36] expressing c•kit no responses were observed. A study conducted in a xenograft model [37] in NCr nude immunodeﬁcient mice showed that therapeutic concentrations of imatinib were achieved in plasma and tumors xenografts, but not in the brain. Moreover imatinib blocked the constitutive activation of c•kit in SCLC tumor cell lines in vitro, with had a negligible effect on SCLC xenograft growth in vitro. At the present time is it not suggested to embark in expensive, time comsuming and potentially ineffective clinical studies on imatinib in SCLC patients [38]. No information is available on the new several ‘superglivec’ which are going to appear on the clinical scenario for the experimental treatment of chronic myeloid leukemia [39]. Temsirolimus (CCI•779) an ester of sirolimus, is a potent inhibitor of mTOR interacting with FKPP• 12 binding protein. The ECOG has conducted a randomized phase II study of two dose levels of temsirolimus: 25 or 250 mg in 87 patients [40] after induction chemotherapy (progressive disease patients excluded). Median progression•free survival was 2.2 months after randomization, with a slight increase in overall survival for the higher dose arm. Further studies with such drug may not be planned in the future. Thalidomide was extensively studied by the Federation National de Centres de Lutte contra le Cancer Group in France. Ninety•two extensive disease patients with PS up to 2, age < 70 years were randomized to placebo or thalidomide 400 mg/daily at the time or response after induction therapy [41]. Overall survival was 8.7 months for the placebo arm and 11.7 for Thalidomide (P = 0.02). This study is a clue in favour of angiogenesis process as a therapeutic window in SCLC therapy, nevertheless the drug proved to be poorly tolerated at the planned dose of 400 mg, and was reduced to 200 mg/daily in half of the patients. More data are certainly warranted. Hu 901•DM 1 (or BB 10901) is an immunoconjugate created by the conjugation of the maytansinoid drug DM 1 to a humanized version of the murine monoclonal antibody N901. BB10901 binds with high afﬁnity to CD56 which is expressed in SCLC. At the dose of 60 mg/sqm/weekly BB 10901 was tested in 10 patients with relapsed SCLC [42] and two obtained a partial remission and another one a minor response. This study is on going up to 35 patients. The story of the inhibition of matrix metalloproteinases (MMP) seems to have come to an end after the phase II randomized study of marimastat which is a synthetic inhibitor of MMP [43]. Fifty hundred and thirty two patients (LD and ED) were randomized to placebo or 10 mg bid orally of marimastat. Median time to progression was 4.4 and 4.3 months respectively. No role for MMP in lung cancer has been observed with two other compound BAY 12–9566 and AG3344 (prinomastat) showing poor results. Another approach tempted is the use of bortezomid (PS•341) which targets the ubiquitin•proteasome pathway interfering with the p21 and p27, p53, cyclins D, E and A, Nuclear Factor k•B, and members of the Bax family [44]. A phase II trial was conducted at the SWOG in 57 ED evaluable patients either responsive or refractory after a platinum combination [45] employing bortezomib at the dose of 1.3 mg/sqm on days 1, 4, 8, 11 q 21 days. Only one partial response was obtained. The SWOG next step with this compound will be the association of bortezomib with topotecan in second line. A totally different approach had been tested by the EORTC; a phase III study of adjuvant vaccination with Bec2/Bacille Calmette Guerin in responding patients with LD SCLC [46]. Bec2 is an anti•idiotipic antibody that mimics GD3, a ganglioside that is expressed on the surface of tumor cells and is of neuroectodermal origin. Overall 515 patients entered this study. Vaccination with Bec2/BCG had no impact on outcome of patients with LD SCLC; median survival from randomization was 16.4 and 14.3 months in the observation and vaccination arms. Anyway a trend towards prolonged survival was observed in the vaccination arm among the patients (one•third) who developed a humoral response. Vaccination strategies in SCLC may still warranted, but using vaccines that produce a better immunologic response. references 1. Chua YJ, Steer C, Yip D.
